metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Pre-calculus 12 is the capstone academic mathematics course, preparing students for calculus and post-secondary mathematics in STEM fields. Students develop deep understanding of function families — through the unifying lenses of inverses, transformations, and function behaviour — including exponential and logarithmic functions, geometric sequences and series, polynomial and rational functions, and advanced trigonometry. This course is required for admission to most post-secondary STEM programs and directly prepares students for Calculus 12.
metadata.general_description: The capstone senior Pre-calculus course covering function transformations, inverses, exponential and logarithmic functions, geometric sequences and series, polynomial and rational functions, and advanced trigonometry — required for post-secondary STEM pathways and Calculus 12.

big_ideas.statement: Using inverses is the foundation to solving equations and describing function relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Inverse operations — logarithms as inverses of exponentials, inverse trigonometric functions, and inverse functions generally — are essential for solving equations and understanding how functions relate to each other.

big_ideas.statement: Understanding function families enables modelling relationships and building connections.
big_ideas.elaboration: Each function family — polynomial, rational, exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric — has characteristic behaviours that make it suitable for modelling different types of real-world relationships. Understanding these families in depth is the foundation of applied mathematics.

big_ideas.statement: Transformations of functions and relations apply across all representations.
big_ideas.elaboration: The geometric transformations of translation, reflection, stretch, and compression apply uniformly to all function families — a powerful unifying principle that reduces the complexity of studying multiple function types.

competencies.raw: Develop thinking strategies to solve puzzles and games; explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas using reason, technology, and tools; estimate reasonably and demonstrate fluent, flexible, strategic thinking about numbers; model mathematics in situational contexts; think creatively with curiosity and wonder.
i_can_statements: I can develop sophisticated algebraic thinking strategies to solve complex function and equation problems.
I can use graphing technology (Desmos, graphing calculator) to explore transformations and function families.
I can estimate and verify the reasonableness of algebraic results, including function values and roots.
I can model real-world phenomena using appropriate function families.

competencies.raw: Develop conceptual understanding through play, story, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore mathematical concepts and relationships; apply flexible and strategic approaches to solve problems; solve with persistence and positive disposition; engage in place-based problem-solving.
i_can_statements: I can develop deep conceptual understanding of function behaviour through inquiry and exploration.
I can visualize function graphs — including asymptotes, discontinuities, and transformations — accurately.
I can apply multiple algebraic strategies — logarithmic solving, polynomial division, trigonometric identities — flexibly.

competencies.raw: Explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions in many ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms; use mathematical vocabulary in discussions; take risks when offering ideas in discourse.
i_can_statements: I can explain and justify algebraic transformations, function analyses, and solution strategies.
I can represent functions accurately in multiple forms: equation, graph, table, and verbal description.
I can use precise Pre-calculus vocabulary: asymptote, discontinuity, period, amplitude, and inverse function.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect mathematical concepts with each other, other areas, and personal interests; use mistakes as opportunities to advance learning; incorporate First Peoples worldviews, perspectives, knowledge, and practices.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my function analysis and algebraic thinking and identify patterns across function families.
I can connect Pre-calculus concepts to physics, chemistry, and computer science applications.
I can incorporate First Peoples mathematical knowledge and perspectives — including cyclical thinking and traditional geometric patterns — into my work.

areas.name: Transformations and Inverses
areas.raw: Transformations of functions and equations; translations, stretches, reflections; inverses.
topics: Transformations of parent functions: y = f(x) → y = af(b(x-h))+k
Translations: horizontal and vertical shifts
Stretches and compressions: horizontal (1/b factor) and vertical (a factor)
Reflections: over x-axis and y-axis
Inverse functions: finding algebraically, graphing y = f⁻¹(x), domain restrictions
Identifying whether a function has an inverse: horizontal line test

areas.name: Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
areas.raw: Exponential functions and equations; logarithms: laws, functions, equations, and applications.
topics: Exponential functions: y = aˣ, base e, growth and decay models
Graphing exponential functions and identifying transformations
Definition of logarithm: y = log_b(x) ↔ b^y = x
Common and natural logarithms: log₁₀ and ln
Laws of logarithms: product, quotient, and power laws
Solving exponential equations using logarithms
Solving logarithmic equations
Applications: compound interest, radioactive decay, pH, decibels, and Richter scale

areas.name: Sequences and Series
areas.raw: Geometric sequences and series; common ratio; infinite series; sigma notation.
topics: Geometric sequences: common ratio r, general term tₙ = t₁ × r^(n-1)
Finite geometric series: Sₙ = t₁(1 - rⁿ)/(1 - r)
Infinite geometric series: S∞ = t₁/(1 - r), conditions for convergence (|r| < 1)
Sigma notation: writing and evaluating series
Applications: compound interest, bouncing ball problems, and fractal perimeters

areas.name: Polynomial and Rational Functions
areas.raw: Polynomial functions and equations; rational functions.
topics: Polynomial functions: end behaviour, degree, leading coefficient, and number of roots
Division algorithm: polynomial long division and synthetic division
Factor theorem: (x - a) is a factor ↔ f(a) = 0
Remainder theorem: f(a) = remainder when f(x) is divided by (x - a)
Integral zero theorem and rational root theorem
Solving polynomial equations: finding all real and complex roots
Rational functions: domain, intercepts, vertical and horizontal asymptotes, holes (discontinuities)
Graphing rational functions with transformations

areas.name: Trigonometry
areas.raw: Trigonometry: angles, unit circle, graphing trig functions, solving equations, identities.
topics: Angles in radians and degrees: conversion, arc length, sector area
Unit circle: coordinates for all special angles (30°, 45°, 60°) in all quadrants
Graphing y = a sin(b(x-h))+k and y = a cos(b(x-h))+k: period, amplitude, phase shift
Graphing y = tan(x) and identifying periodicity and asymptotes
Solving trigonometric equations: general solutions using reference angles
Trigonometric identities: Pythagorean (sin²θ + cos²θ = 1), reciprocal, quotient
Sum and difference identities (optional enrichment)
